pattern: Spreading every source one grid step at a time in lockstep, wave by wave, is exactly multi-source BFS: a cell only ever gets colored on the first wave that reaches it, and among colors arriving in the same wave the maximum one wins.
hints: Think of every source spreading outward one cell per time step simultaneously, like ripples; a cell should only be assigned a color the first time any ripple reaches it.

Run a multi-source BFS starting from all the colored cells at once; process the frontier one full wave at a time so that ties within the same wave can be resolved before any of those cells are marked colored.

For each wave, compute every uncolored neighbor's candidate color as the maximum among all currently-colored neighbors reachable this step, commit all of those cells together, then make them the next frontier.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, two sources on a 3x3 grid
statement example, a source with a much larger color dominates most of the grid
statement example, a single source colors the entire grid
a single cell that is itself the only source
every cell is already a source, so no spreading is needed
a single row (1 x m grid) with one source, a 1-dimensional flood fill
two sources equidistant from a middle cell in a single row: the tie must resolve to the larger color, not the first or nearest one
a single column (n x 1 grid), sources at both ends with the smaller color first
color values at the maximum allowed bound

stress.description: random small-to-moderate grids with a handful of random sources
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    side = max(1, min(size, 40))
    n = rng.randint(1, side)
    m = rng.randint(1, side)
    cells = [(r, c) for r in range(n) for c in range(m)]
    rng.shuffle(cells)
    k = rng.randint(1, len(cells))
    chosen = cells[:k]
    sources = [[r, c, rng.randint(1, 1000000)] for r, c in chosen]
    return [n, m, sources]

stress.description: a long single row with sparse random sources to stress the wide multi-source spread
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    m = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    n = 1
    cols = list(range(m))
    rng.shuffle(cols)
    k = max(1, min(len(cols), 200))
    chosen = sorted(cols[:k])
    sources = [[0, c, rng.randint(1, 1000000)] for c in chosen]
    return [n, m, sources]
